scenarios.name: Specific Performance of Sale Agreement

checks.detail: Specific Relief Act / specific performance cited
At least one statutory section cited
Prayer for execution of sale deed
No invented amounts (10L/50L never in facts)

scenarios.draft_text:
IN THE COURT OF THE CIVIL JUDGE (SENIOR DIVISION)
AT BENGALURU

O.S. No. __________ / 20____

ANITA SHARMA,
Aged about 52 years,
Occupation: Not specified,
Residing at: Indiranagar, Bengaluru.

                                                        ... PLAINTIFF

                    VERSUS

RAJESH REDDY,
Aged about 60 years,
Occupation: Not specified,
Residing at: HSR Layout, Bengaluru.

                                                        ... DEFENDANT

SUIT FOR SPECIFIC PERFORMANCE OF CONTRACT WITH COSTS

MOST RESPECTFULLY SHOWETH:

JURISDICTION

1. The Plaintiff is a person sui juris and competent to institute this suit. The Defendant is a person sui juris and can be sued. The suit property is situated within Bengaluru, and the Defendant resides within HSR Layout, Bengaluru. The contract was executed and was to be performed within the jurisdiction of this Court. Hence, this Court has jurisdiction under Section 20 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908. The suit is filed in the Court of the lowest grade competent to try it, and the suit value is within the pecuniary limits prescribed for this Court under Section 15 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908.

LIMITATION

2. The suit is within limitation under Article 54 of the Schedule to the Limitation Act, 1963, the period prescribed being three years from the date on which the contract was broken. The suit is filed well within the prescribed period.

FACTS OF THE AGREEMENT

3. On 10.01.2024, the Plaintiff entered into an Agreement to Sell with the Defendant for purchase of residential flat No. 302, 3rd Floor, Sunshine Apartments, Koramangala, Bengaluru, measuring 1200 sq ft. The total consideration agreed was Rs. 85,00,000/-.
4. On the date of the agreement, the Plaintiff paid an advance of Rs. 25,00,000/- via RTGS (UTR: HDFC20240110987654). The balance consideration of Rs. 60,00,000/- was to be paid on or before 10.07.2024 against execution of the sale deed.
5. The Agreement to Sell is documented in the original agreement executed by both parties, marked as Annexure A. The payment of advance is evidenced by the RTGS receipt, marked as Annexure B.

PLAINTIFF'S READINESS AND WILLINGNESS

6. The Plaintiff has always been and continues to be ready and willing to perform her part of the contract. The Plaintiff has arranged the balance consideration of Rs. 60,00,000/- and is ready to pay the same against execution of the sale deed.
7. The Plaintiff has taken all necessary steps to perform her obligations, including keeping the funds available and preparing the necessary documents for registration. The Plaintiff has not admitted any inability to perform any term of the agreement.

DEFENDANT'S REFUSAL TO PERFORM

8. On 05.06.2024, the Defendant sent a letter refusing to execute the sale deed, claiming he received a higher offer. This letter is marked as Annexure C.
9. The Plaintiff issued a legal notice dated 20.06.2024 calling upon the Defendant to execute the sale deed. The Defendant refused to comply despite the notice. The legal notice with postal receipt is marked as Annexure D.
10. The Defendant's refusal constitutes a breach of the agreement, entitling the Plaintiff to seek specific performance.

LEGAL BASIS

11. The Plaintiff is entitled to specific performance under Section 10 of the Specific Relief Act, 1963, subject to the provisions of Sections 11(2), 14, and 16 thereof. The contract is valid under Section 10 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872.
12. The Plaintiff has continuously performed her obligations and remains ready and willing as required under Section 16 of the Specific Relief Act, 1963. The Plaintiff seeks compensation under Section 21 of the Specific Relief Act, 1963 in the alternative.
13. The Plaintiff is also entitled to possession under Section 22 of the Specific Relief Act, 1963. The provisions of Section 53A of the Transfer of Property Act, 1882 and Section 17(1A) of the Registration Act, 1908 are applicable to the transaction regarding the agreement to transfer immovable property.

CAUSE OF ACTION

14. The cause of action first arose on 05.06.2024 when the Defendant refused to execute the sale deed despite the agreement dated 10.01.2024.
15. The cause of action continues to subsist as the Defendant has failed to perform his obligations. The suit is filed within the limitation period prescribed under Article 54 of the Limitation Act, 1963.

VALUATION AND COURT FEE

16. The total suit valuation is Rs. 85,00,000/- (Rupees Eighty Five Lakhs Only) for the purpose of jurisdiction and court fee.
17. The court fee amount paid is {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}} as computed under the {{COURT_FEE_ACT_NAME}}. The plaint is properly stamped and tendered with the requisite court fee.

PRAYER

18. In the premises aforesaid, the Plaintiff most respectfully prays that this Hon'ble Court be pleased to:
(a) Pass a decree for specific performance of the Agreement to Sell dated 10.01.2024;
(b) Direct the Defendant to execute the sale deed in favour of the Plaintiff upon payment of the balance consideration;
(c) Direct the Defendant to deliver vacant and peaceful possession of the suit property to the Plaintiff;
(d) In the alternative, award compensation under Section 21 of the Specific Relief Act, 1963 in lieu of or in addition to specific performance, at such amount as this Hon'ble Court deems fit;
(e) Award pre-suit interest if contract, usage, or substantive law permits, pendente lite interest under Section 34 CPC, and future interest under Section 34 CPC as this Hon'ble Court deems fit;
(f) Award costs of the suit to the Plaintiff;
(g) Grant such other and further relief(s) as this Hon'ble Court deems fit and proper.

SCHEDULE OF SUIT PROPERTY

Flat No. 302, 3rd Floor, Sunshine Apartments, Koramangala, Bengaluru, measuring 1200 sq ft.

LIST OF DOCUMENTS

Annexure A — Original Agreement to Sell dated 10.01.2024

Annexure B — RTGS receipt (UTR: HDFC20240110987654)

Annexure C — Defendant's refusal letter dated 05.06.2024

Annexure D — Legal notice dated 20.06.2024 with postal receipt

VERIFICATION

Verified at {{CITY}} on this the ____ day of __________ 20____.

I, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, the Plaintiff above-named, do hereby verify that the contents of paragraphs 1 to 18 of this plaint are true and correct to my knowledge, and no material facts have been concealed therefrom, and that I have not filed any other suit, petition, or proceeding in any court or before any authority in respect of the same subject matter and cause of action.

DEPONENT

{{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}

PLAINTIFF

                                    THROUGH:
                                    {{ADVOCATE_NAME}}
                                    Advocate for the Plaintiff
                                    Enrollment No. {{ENROLLMENT_NO}}
                                    {{ADVOCATE_ADDRESS}}
                                    Mobile: {{ADVOCATE_PHONE}}
                                    Email: {{ADVOCATE_EMAIL}}

scenarios.name: Breach of Contract — Damages (Business)

checks.detail: Total claim Rs.32L or breakdown stated

scenarios.draft_text:
IN THE COURT OF THE COMMERCIAL COURT (COMMERCIAL DIVISION)
AT BENGALURU

C.S. No. __________ / 20____

TechSoft Solutions Pvt Ltd,
A Company registered under the Companies Act,
Having its registered office at: MG Road, Bengaluru.
Represented by its Director, {{PLAINTIFF_REP_NAME}}, aged 40 years.

                                                        ... PLAINTIFF

                    VERSUS

CloudBridge Systems LLP,
A Limited Liability Partnership registered under the LLP Act,
Having its office at: Whitefield, Bengaluru.
Represented by its Designated Partner, {{DEFENDANT_REP_NAME}}, aged 48 years.

                                                        ... DEFENDANT

SUIT FOR RECOVERY OF DAMAGES FOR BREACH OF CONTRACT UNDER SECTION 73 OF THE INDIAN CONTRACT ACT, 1872

MOST RESPECTFULLY SHOWETH:

COMMERCIAL COURT MAINTAINABILITY

1. The subject matter of the present suit arises out of a commercial dispute within the meaning of Section 2(1)(c) of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015. The suit is valued above the specified value of {{AMOUNT}} as prescribed under the Commercial Courts Act, 2015, and is therefore maintainable before this Hon'ble Commercial Court.

JURISDICTION

2. The Plaintiff is a company duly registered under the Companies Act and is a person sui juris competent to institute the present suit. The Defendant is a limited liability partnership registered under the LLP Act and is a person sui juris who can be sued in law.

3. The Contract was executed within the territorial limits of Bengaluru, and the performance was due to be rendered herein. The Defendant maintains its office at Whitefield, Bengaluru, within the jurisdiction of this Hon'ble Court. Thus, this Court has jurisdiction to try the suit under Section 20 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908. The Plaintiff has issued a legal notice dated 01.07.2024 prior to filing. Regarding pre-institution mediation, {{MEDIATION_STATUS}}.

LIMITATION

4. The suit is within limitation under Article 55 of the Schedule to the Limitation Act, 1963, the period prescribed being three years from the date on which the contract was broken. The suit is filed well within the prescribed period.

SECTION 12A COMPLIANCE

5. The Plaintiff states that {{MEDIATION_STATUS}}. Section 12A of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015 compliance is maintained as applicable.

FACTS OF THE CASE

6. On 01.02.2024, the Plaintiff entered into a written Software Development Agreement with the Defendant for the development of an ERP system, as evidenced by Annexure A. The total contract value was agreed at Rs. 45,00,000/-, and the parties were competent to contract, being registered entities acting through their authorised representatives.

7. Pursuant to the terms of the Agreement, the Plaintiff paid the first milestone payment of Rs. 20,00,000/- to the Defendant on 01.02.2024 via NEFT, bearing UTR No. ICIC20240201456789, as evidenced by Annexure B. The Plaintiff has fully performed its payment obligation under the contract.

8. The Defendant was contractually bound to deliver the first module of the ERP system by 01.05.2024. The Plaintiff remained ready and willing to perform its part of the contract and facilitated all necessary access and requirements for the development work.

9. The place of execution of the contract and the place of performance are within Bengaluru. The essential terms included specific delivery timelines and milestone-based payments, which were accepted by both parties at the time of execution.

BREACH OF CONTRACT AND PARTICULARS

10. The Defendant failed to deliver the first module by the agreed deadline of 01.05.2024, constituting a clear breach of the time-bound obligation under the Agreement. No module was delivered by the Defendant up to the date of abandonment.

11. On 15.06.2024, the Defendant unilaterally abandoned the project without completing the deliverables and without returning the advance payment received from the Plaintiff. This abandonment is evidenced by the email correspondence marked as Annexure C.

12. The Plaintiff issued a legal notice dated 01.07.2024 calling upon the Defendant to refund the advance and compensate for losses, marked as Annexure E. The Defendant has failed to respond to the notice or remedy the breach, leaving the Plaintiff with no option but to approach this Hon'ble Court.

LOSS AND DAMAGE

13. The Plaintiff has suffered actual financial loss comprising the refund of the advance payment of Rs. 20,00,000/- paid to the Defendant, which remains unreturned despite the abandonment of the project.

14. The Plaintiff further suffered consequential damages amounting to Rs. 12,00,000/- due to the delay in operations caused by the non-delivery of the ERP system. This loss assessment is supported by the auditor's report marked as Annexure D.

15. The loss claimed was within the contemplation of both parties at the time of contract formation, as the Defendant was aware that the ERP system was critical for the Plaintiff's operational workflow and delay would result in financial impact.

16. The Plaintiff took reasonable steps to mitigate the loss by issuing the legal notice and seeking performance prior to abandonment. However, the Defendant's total repudiation of the contract prevented further mitigation, and the Plaintiff now seeks compensation for the unavoidable loss incurred.

LEGAL BASIS

17. The Plaintiff is entitled to compensation for the breach of contract under Section 73 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872, which mandates compensation for any loss or damage caused by the breach of a contract.

18. Time was of the essence in the present contract given the specific delivery deadline of 01.05.2024, and the failure to perform by this date invokes the principles under Section 55 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872 regarding voidable contracts when time is essential.

19. There has been no novation, accord, or satisfaction of the original contract, and no subsequent agreement has discharged the Defendant's liability to perform or refund the advance amount.

20. The Plaintiff has fulfilled the duty to mitigate loss as required under the proviso to Section 73 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872, and the damages claimed are not remote or speculative but directly flow from the Defendant's breach and abandonment.

CAUSE OF ACTION

21. The cause of action first arose on 15.06.2024 when the Defendant abandoned the project and failed to return the advance payment, giving rise to the Plaintiff's right to sue for recovery and damages.

22. The suit is governed by Article 55 of the Limitation Act, 1963, which prescribes a limitation period of three years from the date the contract is broken. The present suit is filed well within the statutory limitation period.

23. The breach is a single event of repudiation and abandonment, and not a continuing breach, as the Defendant conclusively withdrew from the performance of the contract on 15.06.2024.

VALUATION AND COURT FEE

24. The total suit valuation is Rs. 32,00,000/- (Rupees Thirty-Two Lakhs Only), comprising Rs. 20,00,000/- for refund of advance and Rs. 12,00,000/- for consequential damages, for the purposes of jurisdiction and court fee.

25. The requisite court fee of {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}} has been paid on the plaint under the applicable State Court Fees Act, and the plaint is properly stamped.

26. The suit is valued within the pecuniary limits prescribed for this Hon'ble Commercial Court and is filed in the Court of the lowest grade competent to try the matter.

PRAYER

27. In the premises aforesaid, the Plaintiff most respectfully prays that this Hon'ble Court be pleased to: 
(a) Award damages in favour of the Plaintiff and against the Defendant for Rs. 32,00,000/- (Rupees Thirty-Two Lakhs Only);
(b) Award pendente lite interest at such rate as this Hon'ble Court deems fit from the date of institution of the suit till decree under Order XX Rule 11 CPC;
(c) Award future interest at such rate as this Hon'ble Court deems fit from the date of
